question: Baking out a vacuum system drives off adsorbed water and accelerates H$_2$ outgassing from bulk metal. What is the standard bakeout procedure for a stainless-steel UHV chamber?

options: 150–250°C for 24–72 h while pumping; bake all in-vacuum components; cool slowly ($< 2°$C/min near 120°C); activate ion pump and NEG during cooldown
450°C overnight; cool as fast as possible to lock in the low-outgassing state of the steel
100°C for 1 h — sufficient to remove all outgassing from stainless steel
Bakeout is only needed for the first-ever pump-down; subsequent pump-downs reach the same base pressure without baking
passive.explanation: Bakeout physics: at room temperature, water is strongly adsorbed (sticking coefficient $\sim 1$). Above $\sim 120°$C, water desorption rate greatly exceeds adsorption — baking drives it off. H$_2$ diffuses from the bulk much faster at elevated temperature (diffusivity $\propto e^{-E_a/k_BT}$ with $E_a \approx 0.6$ eV for H in Fe). After bakeout: residual gas dominated by H$_2$ at $< 10^{-10}$ mbar. Rule of thumb: one decade improvement in base pressure per 24 h at 200°C vs unbaked.

active.prompt: Order the correct steps for baking out a new UHV chamber:

steps.text: Assemble chamber with new copper gaskets; connect turbo pump and roughing pump; check all flanges are torqued correctly
Rough pump to $\sim 0.1$ mbar; start turbo pump; rough out to $10^{-6}$ mbar
Wrap chamber in heater tape or place in oven; ramp temperature to 150–250°C at $< 2°$C/min; hold for 24–72 hours; keep turbo running throughout
Ramp temperature back down slowly ($< 2°$C/min below 120°C); at $< 10^{-8}$ mbar and $< 50°$C, start ion pump; activate NEG at 400°C then cool; isolate turbo pump; verify base pressure with BA gauge

active.explanation: Critical points: (1) Never bake with a rotary pump oil connected — it backstreams. (2) Degas the ion gauge during bakeout (electron bombardment heating). (3) Seal the leak valve and blank viewports not in use. (4) NEG activation requires the getter to reach 350–450°C. After cooldown, base pressure should be 10–100× lower than before bakeout.

question: Not all UHV components tolerate the same bakeout temperature. Which combination correctly lists the maximum bakeout temperatures for common components?

options: Stainless steel CF + Cu gaskets 450°C; all-metal valves 250–350°C; Pyrex viewports 200°C; kovar glass-to-metal seals 200–250°C; Viton O-rings 150°C; SMA/BNC feedthroughs 150–200°C
All UHV components can safely be baked to 400°C; only PTFE seals must be removed before bakeout
Viewports must always be excluded from bakeout because they are not UHV-compatible
Copper gaskets are damaged by bakeout and must be replaced after every cycle
passive.explanation: The limiting component sets the whole system's maximum bakeout temperature. Viton O-rings (if present, e.g., on a roughing port) limit the bake to 150°C. All-metal valves with PEEK seats: 200°C. Ion gauges: 250°C (controlled separately). Bake viewports separately at their limit. Rb dispensers tolerate 200°C but should not be activated during bake-in. ITO-coated viewports (for spectroscopy) should not exceed 200°C to avoid coating damage.

question: An RGA (residual gas analyser) is a valuable diagnostic during bakeout. How does the mass spectrum characteristically evolve as the bake progresses?

options: Early: water (18), OH (17), O (16), H (1) dominate; mid: H$_2$ (2) and CO (28) rise as water falls; end: H$_2$ (2) dominates; unexpected peaks (44 CO$_2$, 32 O$_2$, 40 Ar) indicate leaks
Pressure immediately drops to the final base value when the bakeout begins; the mass spectrum is featureless throughout
Water (18 amu) only appears after the bakeout is complete, when the chamber is cooled and re-exposes adsorbed monolayers
CO$_2$ (44 amu) is always the dominant residual gas before, during, and after bakeout
passive.explanation: RGA interpretation: mass 2 = H$_2$ (dominant post-bakeout); 4 = He (leak indicator); 12, 16, 28, 44 = CO/CO$_2$ fragments (contamination or pump oil); 14/28 = N$_2$ fragments (air leak); 18 = H$_2$O (pre-bakeout, surface water); 32 = O$_2$ (air leak when $\sim 1/4$ the N$_2$ peak); 40 = Ar (air leak — Ar is $\sim 1\%$ of air). A clean bakeout ends with only mass 2 visible. Methane (16 amu) can indicate organics or pump oil backstreaming.

question: A titanium sublimation pump (TSP) boosts pumping speed for reactive gases. How does it actually work?

options: A Ti wire is heated to sublimation (50–60 A current); fresh Ti coats the cold chamber walls and chemisorbs H$_2$, N$_2$, CO, O$_2$; fired every few days or hours depending on gas load
Heated Ti block evaporates atoms that are ionised and collected on a biased plate, like a small ion pump
A Ti-coated surface is cooled to liquid-nitrogen temperature to cryopump reactive gases by physisorption
TSP uses photoionisation of background Ti vapour to trap gas molecules at the chamber walls
passive.explanation: TSP parameters: firing current 40–60 A for 1–2 minutes produces a fresh Ti film 5–50 nm thick. Pumping speed for H$_2$: $\sim 8$ L/(s·cm$^2$) of coated surface — a 10 cm diameter TSP shroud gives $\sim 600$ L/s. Does NOT pump noble gases (He, Ar, Ne) or CH$_4$ effectively — only reactive gases. Complementary to NEG (pumps at the NEG surface, no sublimation needed) and ion pumps (pump noble gases via burial). After firing, base pressure drops by 5–10× within minutes, then recovers as the film saturates.

question: In-vacuum optical components must be UHV-compatible and bakeable. Which combination correctly describes the standard choices?

options: Fused silica substrates with ion-beam-sputtered (IBS) coatings; max bakeout $\sim 200°$C (IBS) or 150°C (ion-assisted evaporation); UHV-compatible mounting screws (stainless or titanium)
Any standard coated optic is suitable for UHV if first wiped with acetone and stored in a desiccator before installation
Optical cements (NOA, Norland) are UHV-compatible in small quantities and routinely used in trap viewports
Antireflection coatings must be stripped off the substrate before placing optics in UHV
passive.explanation: UHV optics checklist: (1) Substrate: fused silica preferred ($< 10^{-11}$ mbar·L/(s·cm$^2$) outgassing). BK7 is borderline. (2) Coating: IBS (sputtered, no binders) > e-beam (may contain organics in binder layer). (3) Mounting: contact area minimised to avoid bimetallic stress; Torr Seal epoxy outgassing $\sim 10^{-8}$ mbar·L/s — use sparingly or avoid. (4) Cleaning: ultrasonic IPA/acetone, then drag-wipe with cleanroom lens paper; no bare-hand contact after cleaning. (5) Bakeout: 150–200°C limit for most coated optics.

question: After a successful bakeout, the system is vented only when necessary. What is the best-practice procedure for venting and recovering UHV?

options: Vent slowly with dry nitrogen (not air — avoids water adsorption); work quickly with clean gloves; reseal; rough pump then turbo; bake again at 150°C for 24 h ("mini-bake")
Vent with laboratory air — any gas works since the pump will remove it again
After venting, a full 72-hour bakeout is always required to recover base pressure
Dry-nitrogen venting is no better than air venting since both contain similar outgassing gases at the partial-pressure level
passive.explanation: Why nitrogen: at room temperature, water adsorption on steel is nearly instantaneous (sticking coefficient $\approx 1$ for sub-monolayer coverage). Air at 50% RH contains $\sim 10^{22}$ H$_2$O molecules/m$^3$; in 10 minutes of exposure $\sim 10^{15}$ molecules/cm$^2$ adsorb — a complete monolayer on every surface. Dry N$_2$ (< 1 ppm H$_2$O) reduces adsorbed water by orders of magnitude. Rule of thumb: 1 minute air exposure requires $\sim 2$ hours extra bakeout; dry N$_2$ exposure requires 2–4× less. After a mini-bake, base pressures of $10^{-10}$ mbar typically recovered within 24 h.
